reading.title: The Origins of Tea
reading.passage:
Tea is one of the most widely consumed beverages in the world, second only to water. According to a popular Chinese legend, tea was discovered by accident around 2737 BCE, when leaves from a wild bush blew into a pot of boiling water that the emperor Shen Nong was preparing. Whether or not this story is true, historical records confirm that tea drinking was well established in China long before it reached other parts of the world.

For many centuries, the secret of how to grow and process tea remained within China. Tea was transported along trade routes to neighbouring regions, and it gradually became a valuable item of exchange. By the time European traders arrived in Asia in the sixteenth century, tea was already deeply woven into the culture of several Asian societies.

The Portuguese and the Dutch were among the first Europeans to ship tea back to their home countries, where it was initially regarded as an expensive luxury available only to the wealthy. In Britain, tea did not become popular until the seventeenth century, but once it did, demand grew rapidly. The high price encouraged smuggling, and large quantities of tea entered the country illegally to avoid the heavy taxes imposed by the government.

Eventually, the British began cultivating tea in India in order to reduce their dependence on Chinese supplies. The plantations established there transformed tea from a rare luxury into an everyday drink that ordinary families could afford. Today, tea is produced in dozens of countries, and each region has developed its own distinctive varieties and traditions of preparation.

questions.q: Tea is consumed more than any other drink except water.
Knowledge of how to grow and process tea stayed within China for many centuries.
The Portuguese were the people who first introduced tea to China.
Tea became popular in Britain as soon as it first arrived there.
Smuggling of tea happened because of the high taxes placed on it.
The British began growing tea in ________ in order to depend less on Chinese supplies.
According to the passage, the tea plantations in India led to tea becoming:

listening.title: Joining the City Library
listening.script:
WOMAN: Good morning, I'd like to join the city library. What do I need to do?
MAN: Of course. I'll just take a few details. Can I have your full name?
WOMAN: Yes, it's Karen Mitchell. That's M-I-T-C-H-E-L-L.
MAN: Thank you. And your date of birth?
WOMAN: The twelfth of March, 1995.
MAN: Lovely. What's your current address?
WOMAN: It's forty-seven Park Avenue.
MAN: Now, there are two types of membership. The standard card is free and lets you borrow up to six books for three weeks. The premium card costs fifteen pounds a year and allows you to borrow twelve items, including DVDs.
WOMAN: I'll take the premium one, please.
MAN: No problem. The library is open from nine to eight on weekdays, but it closes early at five on Saturdays. We're closed on Sundays.
WOMAN: Good to know. Thank you so much.

questions.q: Surname: ________
Date of birth: 12th of ________ 1995
Address: ________ Park Avenue
Which membership does the woman choose?
Premium membership costs £________ a year.
On Saturdays the library closes at ________ o'clock.

speaking.q: Let's talk about your hometown. Where is it, and what do you like most about living there? Speak for about one to two minutes.

writing.q: Some people believe that children should begin learning a foreign language at primary school, while others think it is better to start in secondary school. Discuss both views and give your own opinion. Write at least 250 words.

reading.title: Urban Green Spaces
reading.passage:
As cities around the world continue to expand, urban planners are paying increasing attention to the role of green spaces such as parks, gardens and tree-lined streets. These areas were once viewed mainly as places for recreation, but research has shown that they provide a far wider range of benefits to city dwellers.

One of the most significant advantages of urban greenery is its effect on temperature. During hot weather, densely built areas can become noticeably warmer than the surrounding countryside, a phenomenon known as the urban heat island effect. Trees and plants help to cool the air by providing shade and releasing moisture, which can lower local temperatures by several degrees.

Green spaces also contribute to cleaner air. Vegetation absorbs certain pollutants and traps tiny particles that would otherwise be breathed in by residents. In addition, parks encourage physical activity, as people are more likely to walk, run or cycle when attractive outdoor spaces are available nearby.

There are psychological benefits too. Studies have repeatedly found that spending time in natural surroundings reduces stress and improves concentration. Some hospitals have even reported that patients with a view of greenery from their windows tend to recover more quickly than those without.

Despite these benefits, creating and maintaining green spaces can be costly, and land in city centres is often extremely valuable. Planners must therefore balance the demand for housing and commercial development against the long-term advantages that parks and gardens bring to a community.

questions.q: In the past, green spaces were mainly considered places for recreation.
The urban heat island effect makes city centres cooler than the surrounding countryside.
Plants can reduce local temperatures by providing shade and releasing moisture.
All hospitals now include gardens for their patients.
Vegetation absorbs pollutants and traps tiny ________ in the air.
Research shows that time in nature reduces stress and improves ________.
According to the passage, planners must:
options: ban all new housing in cities
balance development against the benefits of green spaces
move all parks to the countryside
reduce the value of city-centre land

listening.title: Student Accommodation Enquiry
listening.script:
STUDENT: Hi, I'm looking for accommodation near the university for next term.
OFFICER: Sure. Are you interested in a hall of residence or a private flat?
STUDENT: A hall of residence, I think. It's my first year.
OFFICER: Good choice. We have two main halls. Riverside Hall is the closest, just five minutes' walk from campus, and a single room there costs ninety pounds a week. Greenfield Hall is a bit further, about twenty minutes away, but it's cheaper at seventy-five pounds a week.
STUDENT: Does the price include meals?
OFFICER: At Riverside, breakfast and dinner are included. Greenfield is self-catered.
STUDENT: And is there internet?
OFFICER: Yes, both halls have free wireless internet in every room.
STUDENT: What about laundry?
OFFICER: There's a laundry room in each building. It costs two pounds per wash.
STUDENT: Great. I think I'll go for Riverside Hall.
OFFICER: Excellent. I'll send you the application form by email today.

questions.q: What type of accommodation does the student choose?
Riverside Hall is ________ minutes' walk from campus.
A single room at Greenfield Hall costs £________ a week.
Which meals are included at Riverside Hall?
Using the laundry costs £________ per wash.
The student finally chooses ________ Hall.

speaking.q: Describe a skill you would like to learn. You should say: what the skill is, why you want to learn it, and how you would learn it; and explain how this skill would be useful to you. You have one minute to prepare, then speak for two minutes.

writing.q:
The table below shows the percentage of workers who travelled to work by public transport in four cities in 2000 and 2020. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.

DATA (% using public transport):
- City A — 2000: 35%, 2020: 52%
- City B — 2000: 48%, 2020: 45%
- City C — 2000: 20%, 2020: 38%
- City D — 2000: 60%, 2020: 66%

reading.title: The Science of Sleep
reading.passage:
Sleep is something every human being needs, yet for a long time scientists understood very little about why it is so important. Early researchers assumed that sleep was simply a period of rest, during which the body did almost nothing. Modern studies, however, have revealed that the brain remains remarkably active while we sleep.

A typical night's sleep is divided into several cycles, each lasting around ninety minutes. Within each cycle, the brain moves through different stages, including a phase known as REM, or rapid eye movement, sleep. It is during REM sleep that most vivid dreaming occurs. Researchers believe this stage plays an essential part in processing emotions and storing memories from the previous day.

The amount of sleep a person needs changes with age. Newborn babies may sleep for sixteen hours a day, whereas most adults function best with around seven to eight hours. Regularly getting too little sleep has been linked to a range of problems, including poor concentration, weakened immunity and an increased risk of certain illnesses.

Despite this knowledge, many people in modern societies do not get enough rest. Bright screens, busy schedules and artificial lighting can all interfere with the body's natural sleep patterns. Experts recommend keeping a regular bedtime, avoiding electronic devices before sleep, and ensuring that the bedroom is dark and quiet. Such simple measures, they argue, can greatly improve both the quality and the quantity of sleep that people obtain.

questions.q: Early researchers believed the body was almost inactive during sleep.
Each sleep cycle lasts about ninety minutes.
Most vivid dreaming happens outside REM sleep.
REM sleep is thought to help with processing emotions and storing ________.
Most adults function best with around seven to ________ hours of sleep.
Everyone in modern societies gets enough sleep.
Which of the following is NOT recommended by experts?
options: using electronic devices just before sleep

listening.title: Lecture: How Volcanoes Form
listening.script:
Good afternoon, everyone. Today we're going to look at volcanoes and how they form. A volcano is essentially an opening in the Earth's surface through which molten rock, gases and ash can escape. Beneath the surface, this molten rock is called magma, but once it reaches the surface and flows out, we refer to it as lava.

Most volcanoes are found along the edges of the giant plates that make up the Earth's outer layer. There are roughly fifteen hundred active volcanoes in the world today, and a large number of these are located around the Pacific Ocean, in a region often called the Ring of Fire.

Volcanic eruptions can be extremely destructive, burying entire towns and affecting the climate for months. However, they also bring benefits. Volcanic soil is exceptionally fertile, which is why many farming communities choose to live nearby despite the risks.

questions.q: Molten rock beneath the Earth's surface is called ________.
When molten rock flows out onto the surface it is called ________.
There are roughly ________ active volcanoes in the world today.
Many Pacific volcanoes lie in a region called the Ring of ________.
Why do many farming communities live near volcanoes?
The lecturer says volcanic eruptions bring no benefits at all.

speaking.q: We've been talking about technology. Some people say modern technology has made everyday life too complicated. To what extent do you agree, and how do you think people can manage technology in a healthier way? Develop your answer with reasons and examples.

writing.q: In many countries, the number of people choosing to live alone is increasing. What do you think are the causes of this trend, and what effects does it have on society? Write at least 250 words.

reading.title: Honeybees and Pollination
reading.passage:
Honeybees are among the most important insects on the planet, not because of the honey they produce, but because of the vital role they play in pollination. Pollination is the process by which pollen is transferred from one flower to another, allowing plants to produce seeds and fruit. As bees move from flower to flower collecting nectar, pollen sticks to their bodies and is carried along with them.

It is estimated that a significant proportion of the crops grown for human consumption depend, at least in part, on pollination by insects. Without bees and other pollinators, many fruits, vegetables and nuts would become far more difficult and expensive to produce. For this reason, the health of bee populations is closely linked to global food security.

In recent decades, however, beekeepers in many countries have reported alarming declines in the number of bees. Scientists have identified several possible causes, including the use of certain pesticides, the loss of wild flowers, the spread of diseases and the effects of a changing climate. It is likely that these factors act in combination rather than alone.

Efforts to protect bees are now under way in many regions. Some governments have restricted the use of harmful chemicals, while individuals are being encouraged to plant flowers that provide food for pollinators. Researchers stress that protecting bees is not simply a matter of preserving a single species, but of safeguarding the complex natural systems on which human life ultimately depends.

questions.q: Honeybees are important mainly because of the honey they make.
Pollination allows plants to produce seeds and ________.
Many crops grown for human food depend partly on insect pollination.
Scientists are certain that one single factor is responsible for the decline in bees.
Which of the following is mentioned as a possible cause of bee decline?
Individuals are encouraged to plant ________ that provide food for pollinators.
Some governments have restricted the use of harmful chemicals.

listening.title: Booking a Guided Tour
listening.script:
AGENT: Good morning, City Tours, how can I help you?
CUSTOMER: Hi, I'd like to book a guided walking tour of the old town for this weekend.
AGENT: Certainly. We run tours on both Saturday and Sunday. Which day would you prefer?
CUSTOMER: Saturday, please.
AGENT: We have two times on Saturday, one at ten in the morning and one at two in the afternoon.
CUSTOMER: The morning one sounds good.
AGENT: Great. The tour lasts about two hours and the meeting point is in front of the Town Hall. How many people will be coming?
CUSTOMER: There'll be four of us, two adults and two children.
AGENT: The price is twelve pounds for adults and six pounds for children, so that comes to thirty-six pounds in total.
CUSTOMER: That's fine. Is there anything we should bring?
AGENT: I'd recommend comfortable shoes and a bottle of water. And please arrive ten minutes early.
CUSTOMER: Will do. Thank you very much.

questions.q: Which day does the customer book the tour for?
The morning tour starts at ________ o'clock.
The tour lasts about ________ hours.
The meeting point is in front of the ________ Hall.
The total price for the whole group is £________.
Customers are asked to arrive ________ minutes early.

speaking.q: Let's talk about food. What kinds of food do you enjoy eating, and do you prefer cooking at home or eating out? Explain why.

writing.q:
The chart below shows the number of visitors (in thousands) to a national museum across four seasons in two different years, 2015 and 2022. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.

DATA (visitors, in thousands):
- Spring — 2015: 40, 2022: 55
- Summer — 2015: 70, 2022: 90
- Autumn — 2015: 35, 2022: 50
- Winter — 2015: 25, 2022: 30

reading.title: The History of Lighthouses
reading.passage:
For thousands of years, sailors have relied on lights along the coast to guide them safely to harbour and to warn them of dangerous rocks. The earliest lighthouses were little more than open fires built on hilltops, but over time they developed into the tall, purpose-built towers that we recognise today.

One of the most famous structures of the ancient world was the lighthouse of Alexandria, in Egypt, which was completed more than two thousand years ago. Standing at a remarkable height, it was considered an extraordinary feat of engineering and remained in use for many centuries before it was eventually destroyed by earthquakes.

For most of their history, lighthouses depended on fire or oil lamps for their light. The major challenge was making the light visible from a great distance. This problem was largely solved in the early nineteenth century with the invention of a special lens that could focus a beam of light far out to sea. This invention dramatically improved the effectiveness of lighthouses around the world.

Traditionally, each lighthouse was operated by a keeper, who lived on site and was responsible for maintaining the light through the night. The work could be lonely and demanding, particularly in remote locations. In the twentieth century, however, advances in technology made it possible to operate lighthouses automatically. As a result, the role of the lighthouse keeper has now almost entirely disappeared, although the structures themselves continue to play an important part in keeping sailors safe.

questions.q: The earliest lighthouses were tall, purpose-built towers.
The famous ancient lighthouse was located in ________, in Egypt.
The lighthouse of Alexandria was eventually destroyed by earthquakes.
A special ________ invented in the early nineteenth century could focus a beam of light far out to sea.
What was the main challenge for early lighthouses?
options: making the light visible from a great distance

questions.q: Lighthouse keepers remain essential to operating lighthouses today.
In the twentieth century, technology made it possible to operate lighthouses ________.

listening.title: Lecture: Techniques for Improving Memory
listening.script:
Hello everyone. In today's session, I want to share a few practical techniques for improving your memory. The first technique is called chunking. This simply means breaking a long piece of information, such as a phone number, into smaller groups so that it is easier to remember.

The second technique involves using visual images. Our brains are very good at remembering pictures, so if you connect a piece of information to a strong mental image, you are far more likely to recall it later.

Finally, there is the power of repetition, but with a twist. Rather than reviewing material many times in a single session, it is much more effective to spread your review over several days. This method, known as spaced repetition, helps move information into your long-term memory. If you apply these three techniques regularly, you should notice a real improvement within a few weeks.

questions.q: The first memory technique is called ________.
Chunking means breaking information into smaller ________.
Why does the speaker say visual images help memory?
options: because brains are good at remembering pictures
because images are easy to draw

questions.q: Spreading your review over several days is known as ________ repetition.
The speaker recommends reviewing all material in one single session.
With regular practice, improvement should appear within a few ________.

speaking.q: Describe a memorable journey you have taken. You should say: where you went, who you went with, and what you did during the journey; and explain why it was so memorable. You have one minute to prepare, then speak for two minutes.

writing.q: Some people think that governments should spend money on public services such as health and education rather than on the arts, such as music and theatre. To what extent do you agree or disagree? Write at least 250 words.

reading.title: The Origins of Writing
reading.passage:
Writing is one of humanity's most powerful inventions, yet it appeared surprisingly late in our history. For tens of thousands of years, people relied entirely on speech and memory to pass on knowledge. The earliest known writing systems emerged only about five thousand years ago, in a small number of ancient societies.

Many scholars believe that writing was first developed not for literature or for recording stories, but for far more practical reasons. In ancient Mesopotamia, for example, the first written symbols seem to have been used to keep track of trade: how much grain had been stored, or how many animals a person owned. Only later did writing come to be used for laws, religion and poetry.

Different cultures invented writing independently. The systems of ancient Egypt, China and Central America developed separately, each with its own unique set of symbols. This suggests that writing was not a single accident but something that human societies tend to create once they reach a certain level of complexity.

The spread of writing had enormous consequences. It allowed information to be stored accurately and passed across great distances and long periods of time. A message could now outlive the person who wrote it. Some thinkers have argued that without writing, the large, organised states of the ancient world could never have existed, because rulers would have had no reliable way to manage taxes, armies and trade.

questions.q: Writing appeared early in human history.
The earliest known writing systems are about five thousand years old.
Most scholars believe writing was first used mainly for poetry and stories.
In Mesopotamia, the first written symbols were used to keep track of ________.
The writing systems of Egypt, China and Central America were copied from one another.
What does the independent invention of writing in different cultures suggest?
options: societies tend to create writing once they reach a certain complexity
only one culture was ever able to write

questions.q: Writing allowed a message to ________ the person who wrote it.

listening.title: Gym Membership Enquiry
listening.script:
RECEPTIONIST: Good afternoon, City Fitness, how can I help?
CALLER: Hi, I'd like to ask about joining the gym.
RECEPTIONIST: Of course. We have two memberships. The standard plan is twenty-five pounds a month and gives you access to the gym floor. The premium plan is forty pounds a month and includes all the classes as well.
CALLER: What kind of classes do you offer?
RECEPTIONIST: We have yoga, swimming and cycling classes. The most popular one is yoga on Tuesday evenings.
CALLER: Is there a joining fee?
RECEPTIONIST: There's a one-off fee of ten pounds, but it's free if you join before the end of the month.
CALLER: Great, I'll join now then. And what are the opening hours?
RECEPTIONIST: We're open from six in the morning until ten at night on weekdays, and eight to six at weekends.
CALLER: Could I book a tour for tomorrow?
RECEPTIONIST: Certainly. Please bring a photo ID when you come.

questions.q: The standard plan costs £________ a month.
What does the premium plan include that the standard plan does not?
The most popular class is ________ on Tuesday evenings.
The joining fee is normally £________.
On weekdays the gym opens at ________ in the morning.
Customers should bring a photo ________ when they visit.

speaking.q: Let's talk about reading. Do you enjoy reading books, and what kind of books do you prefer? Has your reading changed since you were a child?

writing.q:
The table below shows the number of books (in thousands) borrowed from a city library in four categories in 2010 and 2020. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.

DATA (books borrowed, in thousands):
- Fiction — 2010: 120, 2020: 95
- Children's — 2010: 80, 2020: 110
- Non-fiction — 2010: 60, 2020: 70
- E-books — 2010: 10, 2020: 90

vocab.example: Writing is a powerful invention.
People relied on memory to pass on knowledge.
Writing systems emerged five thousand years ago.
Writing began for practical reasons.
Symbols kept track of grain and animals.
Cultures invented writing independently.
A society reaches a certain complexity.
Writing had enormous consequences.
Information could be stored accurately.
A message can outlive its writer.

reading.title: The Benefits of Bilingualism
reading.passage:
For much of the twentieth century, many people believed that growing up with two languages might confuse a child and slow down their development. Today, research paints a very different picture. Far from being a disadvantage, being bilingual appears to bring a range of mental benefits.

One of the most studied advantages concerns what psychologists call executive function — the brain's ability to plan, focus attention and switch between tasks. Because bilingual people constantly choose between two languages and suppress the one they are not using, their brains get a kind of regular exercise. Several studies suggest that this can lead to better concentration and mental flexibility.

There may also be benefits later in life. Some research has found that bilingual adults show the symptoms of certain age-related memory conditions several years later than people who speak only one language. Scientists stress that being bilingual does not prevent such conditions, but it may help the brain cope with them for longer.

Not all researchers agree on the size of these effects, and some earlier findings have been difficult to repeat. Nevertheless, there is wide agreement that learning and using more than one language is valuable. Beyond any mental benefits, speaking another language allows people to communicate with more of the world and to understand other cultures more deeply.

questions.q: In the past, some people thought two languages might confuse a child.
Modern research supports the old view that bilingualism slows development.
Bilingual people must constantly ________ the language they are not using.
What is 'executive function'?
options: the ability to speak two languages
the brain's ability to plan, focus and switch tasks

questions.q: Being bilingual completely prevents age-related memory conditions.
All researchers agree on exactly how large the benefits are.
Speaking another language helps people understand other ________ more deeply.

listening.title: Welcome to the University Library
listening.script:
Hello and welcome to the university library. Let me quickly explain how things work. The library has four floors. The ground floor is where you'll find the help desk and the photocopiers. The first floor is for newspapers and magazines, and it's also our designated quiet zone, so please don't talk on that floor.

Most of the academic books are kept on the second floor, arranged by subject. If you can't find what you need, the staff will be happy to help. The top floor has group study rooms, which you can book online for up to two hours at a time.

As a student, you can borrow up to ten books at once, and you can keep them for three weeks. If you return a book late, there's a small fine of twenty pence per day. One last thing: the library closes at midnight during term time, but at weekends it closes earlier, at nine in the evening. Please make sure you take all your belongings with you when you leave.

questions.q: The help desk is on the ________ floor.
The first floor is a designated ________ zone.
Academic books on the second floor are arranged by ________.
Students can borrow up to ________ books at once.
The late fine is ________ pence per day.
When does the library close at weekends?

speaking.q: Describe a book you have read that you found useful. You should say: what the book was, why you read it, and what you learned from it; and explain why you found it useful. You have one minute to prepare, then speak for two minutes.

writing.q: Some people think the best way to learn a new language is to live in a country where it is spoken. Others believe it can be learned just as well at home. Discuss both views and give your own opinion. Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: Bilingual children switch between two languages.
It was once seen as a disadvantage.
They suppress the language they are not using.
Bilingualism may improve mental flexibility.
Symptoms may appear several years later.
The brain copes with the condition for longer.
There may be cognitive benefits.
Nevertheless, learning a language is valuable.
Using more than one language is valuable.
You understand other cultures more deeply.

reading.title: How Coral Reefs Survive
reading.passage:
Coral reefs are often described as the rainforests of the sea. Although they cover only a tiny fraction of the ocean floor, they are home to a remarkable share of all marine species. The structure of a reef is built by tiny animals called coral polyps, which produce hard skeletons of calcium carbonate over thousands of years.

What makes corals especially interesting is their close relationship with tiny algae that live inside their tissues. The algae use sunlight to produce food, much of which they share with the coral. In return, the coral provides the algae with shelter and the chemicals they need. This partnership is so important that without it most corals could not survive.

Unfortunately, this relationship is also fragile. When the water becomes too warm, the corals expel the algae living inside them, turning white in a process known as bleaching. A bleached coral is not yet dead, but if the warm conditions continue for too long, it will starve. Rising ocean temperatures have already caused severe bleaching events in reefs around the world.

Scientists are now searching for ways to protect reefs, from breeding corals that can tolerate warmer water to creating protected areas where fishing is limited. Many experts warn, however, that the most important step is to slow the warming of the oceans itself, since local efforts alone cannot save reefs if the seas keep heating up.

questions.q: Coral reefs cover a large fraction of the ocean floor.
Reefs are built by tiny animals called coral ________.
Both the algae and the coral benefit from their relationship.
When water is too warm, corals expel their algae and turn white in a process called ________.
A coral dies immediately as soon as it bleaches.
What do many experts say is the most important step to protect reefs?
options: slowing the warming of the oceans

questions.q: Local efforts alone are enough to save reefs.

listening.title: Choosing a Research Topic
listening.script:
TUTOR: So, have you decided on a topic for your project yet?
STUDENT: I'm still not sure. I'm interested in renewable energy, but it feels too broad.
TUTOR: That's a good instinct. A common mistake is choosing a topic that's far too wide. You'll get a much better result if you narrow it down.
STUDENT: Maybe I could focus just on solar power?
TUTOR: Better, but still quite large. What about looking at how solar power is used in one particular city or country?
STUDENT: That's a good idea. I could compare two cities.
TUTOR: Exactly. Comparisons often make strong projects. Now, how will you collect your information?
STUDENT: Mostly from journal articles and government reports, I think.
TUTOR: Good. Just remember to keep a careful record of your sources as you go. Students often lose marks simply because they forget where they found something.
STUDENT: I'll make a note of everything. When is the first draft due?
TUTOR: In four weeks. Send me an outline before then if you'd like feedback.

questions.q: What does the tutor say is a common mistake?
options: choosing a topic that is too narrow
choosing a topic that is too wide

questions.q: The student decides to compare two ________.
Information will come from journal articles and government ________.
The tutor says students often lose marks because they forget their ________.
The first draft is due in ________ weeks.
The tutor offers to give feedback on an outline.

speaking.q: We've been talking about the environment. Some people believe protecting nature is mainly the responsibility of governments, while others think individuals must act too. What do you think, and what can ordinary people realistically do? Develop your answer with reasons and examples.

writing.q:
The graph below shows the average monthly temperature (in °C) in two cities, Cityville and Townton, at four points across one year. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.

DATA (average temperature, °C):
- Cityville — January: 4, April: 12, July: 22, October: 13
- Townton — January: -2, April: 9, July: 25, October: 10

vocab.example: Reefs cover a tiny fraction of the sea floor.
They host a remarkable share of species.
The coral provides shelter for the algae.
The relationship is also fragile.
Warm water makes corals expel their algae.
Some corals can tolerate warmer water.
There have been severe bleaching events.
A bleached coral may eventually starve.
Scientists breed corals that tolerate heat.
Fishing is limited in protected areas.

reading.title: The Story of Coffee Houses
reading.passage:
Today coffee is enjoyed all over the world, but the social spaces built around it have a long and surprising history. The first coffee houses appeared in the Middle East several centuries ago, and they quickly became popular places for people to meet, talk and exchange news.

When coffee houses spread to Europe in the seventeenth century, they had a similar effect. In cities such as London, they became known as places where people from different backgrounds could sit together and discuss the issues of the day. For the price of a single cup, a customer could read the latest newspapers and join in lively conversation. Some coffee houses became so closely linked with particular trades or interests that merchants and writers would gather at the same one again and again.

Not everyone approved. Some rulers worried that these gatherings encouraged dangerous political discussion, and there were occasional attempts to close them down. Such efforts rarely succeeded for long, however, because coffee houses had become too popular and too useful.

In many ways, the early coffee house played a role that we might now associate with the internet: it was a place to share information rapidly, to debate, and to do business. While the coffee houses of the past have largely disappeared in their original form, the idea of the café as a centre of social life has never gone away.

questions.q: The first coffee houses appeared in Europe.
Coffee houses spread to Europe in the ________ century.
In London, coffee houses brought together people from different backgrounds.
Why did some rulers want to close coffee houses?
options: they worried about dangerous political discussion

questions.q: Attempts to close coffee houses usually succeeded in the long term.
The writer compares the early coffee house to the modern ________.
The idea of the café as a social centre has disappeared completely.

listening.title: Reporting a Lost Item
listening.script:
STAFF: Hello, lost property office. How can I help?
CALLER: Hi, I think I left my bag on the train this morning.
STAFF: I'm sorry to hear that. Let me take some details. Can you describe the bag?
CALLER: It's a medium-sized backpack, dark blue, with a side pocket.
STAFF: And what was inside it?
CALLER: A laptop, a notebook, and an umbrella.
STAFF: Which train were you on?
CALLER: The eight fifteen from Riverton to Capeton.
STAFF: Thank you. And can I take your name?
CALLER: It's Daniel Frost.
STAFF: Lovely. If we find it, we'll call you. You can also collect lost items from our office, which is open from nine to five.
CALLER: How long do you keep things for?
STAFF: We hold lost property for one month before donating it to charity.
CALLER: Okay, thank you very much for your help.

questions.q: The lost bag is a dark blue ________.
Inside the bag were a laptop, a notebook and an ________.
The train the caller took left Riverton at ________.
The caller's surname is ________.
The office is open from nine to ________.
Lost property is kept for one ________ before being donated.

speaking.q: Let's talk about drinks. What do you usually drink during the day? Do you prefer tea, coffee, or something else, and why?

writing.q: Some people believe that social media has had a mostly positive effect on society, while others think it has done more harm than good. Discuss both views and give your own opinion. Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: People met to exchange news.
People from different backgrounds met there.
They joined in lively conversation.
Merchants gathered at the same coffee house.

reading.title: Why We Yawn
reading.passage:
Almost everyone yawns, and so do many animals, yet scientists still do not fully agree on why we do it. A yawn involves opening the mouth wide, taking a long breath, and then breathing out. It seems simple, but explaining its purpose has proved surprisingly difficult.

For many years, the most popular idea was that yawning increases the amount of oxygen in the blood. According to this theory, we yawn when we are tired or bored because our breathing has become shallow. However, careful experiments have cast doubt on this explanation, since giving people extra oxygen does not appear to stop them yawning.

A more recent theory suggests that yawning helps to cool the brain. When we yawn, the deep breath and the stretching of the jaw may increase blood flow and bring in cooler air, lowering the temperature of the brain slightly. Some studies have found that people yawn more in warm conditions than in cold ones, which seems to support this idea.

There is also the curious fact that yawning is contagious. Seeing, hearing, or even reading about yawning can make us yawn. Many scientists think this is connected to empathy — our tendency to share the feelings and behaviour of others. Whatever the true explanation, yawning remains a small everyday mystery that reminds us how much we still have to learn about the human body.

questions.q: Scientists completely agree on why people yawn.
The older theory said yawning increases the amount of ________ in the blood.
Experiments showed that giving people extra oxygen stops them yawning.
What does the more recent theory suggest yawning does?
People appear to yawn more in warm conditions than in cold ones.
Many scientists link contagious yawning to ________.
Even ________ about yawning can make us yawn.

listening.title: Introduction to Recycling
listening.script:
Good morning. In this short talk I'll introduce how a typical recycling system works. When you put items into a recycling bin, they don't simply turn into new products by magic. The first stage is collection, when trucks take the materials to a sorting centre.

At the sorting centre, the mixed materials are separated. Some of this is done by machines, which use magnets to pull out metal and jets of air to separate light materials like paper. The rest is often sorted by hand. Once separated, each type of material is cleaned and pressed into large blocks called bales.

These bales are then sold to factories, where they are turned into raw material for new products. For example, recycled glass can be melted down and made into new bottles again and again without losing quality. Paper, on the other hand, can only be recycled a limited number of times, because the fibres become shorter and weaker each time. In the next lecture, we'll look at what happens to plastic in more detail.

questions.q: The first stage of recycling is ________.
At the sorting centre, machines use ________ to pull out metal.
Separated materials are pressed into large blocks called ________.
What does the speaker say about recycled glass?
options: it can be made into new bottles again and again
it is sorted only by hand

questions.q: Paper can be recycled an unlimited number of times.
The next lecture will focus on ________.

speaking.q: Describe a useful object in your home. You should say: what it is, how you got it, and how often you use it; and explain why it is so useful. You have one minute to prepare, then speak for two minutes.

writing.q:
The table below shows the percentage of household waste that was recycled in three towns in 2005, 2012 and 2019. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.

DATA (% of waste recycled):
- Town A — 2005: 18%, 2012: 35%, 2019: 52%
- Town B — 2005: 25%, 2012: 30%, 2019: 41%
- Town C — 2005: 12%, 2012: 28%, 2019: 60%

vocab.example: We yawn when we are tired.
Our breathing becomes shallow when bored.
Experiments cast doubt on the theory.
A more recent theory exists.
It lowers the temperature slightly.
Yawning is contagious.
Empathy is our tendency to share feelings.
The materials are separated at the centre.
Bales become raw material for products.
Paper fibres become shorter each time.
